title: Group Decision-Making
summary: How teams generate options and commit to a choice — decision rules, Delphi, and the quick consensus tools agile teams use.

sections.heading: How groups decide (decision rules)
sections.body:
When a group must commit to a choice, the decision rule sets the bar for agreement:

- Unanimity — everyone agrees. Strong buy-in but slow; techniques like Delphi and fist of five help reach it.
- Majority — more than half agree (over 50%). Common and quick.
- Plurality — the largest single block decides, even if it is under half; used when several options split the vote and no one has a majority.
- Autocratic — one individual decides on behalf of the group. Fastest, but the weakest buy-in.

Most agile teams aim for consensus — broad agreement everyone can live with and support — rather than strict unanimity.
sections.tip: Plurality = the biggest group wins (no majority needed). Majority = over half. Don't confuse them.

sections.heading: Generating options first
sections.body:
Good decisions start with a wide set of ideas, generated before any are judged:

- Brainstorming — produce many ideas freely, deferring all criticism until later.
- Nominal group technique — brainstorm, then privately rank the ideas, so louder voices don't dominate the outcome.
- Mind mapping — branch related ideas out from a central theme to reveal structure and links.
- Affinity diagram — cluster a large pile of ideas into natural groupings to make sense of them.
sections.tip: Nominal group technique = brainstorming PLUS private voting, so the loudest person in the room doesn't win.

sections.heading: Delphi & weighted decision matrices
sections.body:
Two structured techniques remove bias from harder judgements:

- Delphi technique — a panel of experts responds in anonymous rounds; a facilitator summarises each round and feeds it back until the answers converge. Anonymity removes seniority bias, bandwagon effects and groupthink. Used for estimates, risk and forecasts.
- Multi-criteria decision analysis (a weighted decision matrix) — score each option against weighted criteria such as cost, risk and value, then total the weighted scores to compare options objectively rather than on gut feel.
sections.tip: Delphi = anonymous + iterative + expert. The anonymity is the whole point — it kills bias and bandwagons.

sections.heading: Quick agile consensus tools
sections.body:
Agile teams use fast, visible techniques to read the room:

- Fist of five (fist to five) — on a count, everyone shows zero to five fingers: a fist (0) is a block or strong objection, 5 is enthusiastic support, 3 means "I can live with it". Anything below 3 is discussed before proceeding.
- Roman voting — thumbs up, sideways or down for an instant read of agreement.
- Dot voting — each person places a fixed number of dots on the options they favour.
- Planning poker — estimators reveal their cards at the same moment, so no one anchors on the first number spoken.
sections.tip: Fist of five: a 1 or 2 means "I have concerns" — keep discussing; you don't proceed until everyone is at 3 or above.

figure.caption: Fist of five: 0 blocks, 3 is 'can live with it', 5 is full support.

sections.heading: Choosing the approach
sections.body:
Match the technique to the decision:

- High stakes that need buy-in — aim for consensus or unanimity, accepting it is slower.
- Routine or time-boxed calls — majority, plurality, or let the accountable person decide.
- Expert judgement under uncertainty — use Delphi.
- Comparing options across several factors — use a weighted decision matrix.

Whatever rule you use, agree it before the discussion starts, so everyone accepts the outcome it produces.
sections.tip: Agree the decision rule up front. Changing the rule after you see the result destroys trust.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
